spans.content: Size Dependence of Grain Boundary Migration in Metals under Mechanical Loading
Xin Zhou,1,2 Xiuyan Li,1,*
and K. Lu1,†
1 Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences,
72 Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China
2 School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China
(Received 29 November 2018; revised manuscript received 23 January 2019; published 28 March 2019)
The greatly increased grain boundary (GB) mobility in nanograined metals under mechanical loading is
distinguished from that in their coarse-grained counterparts. The feature leads to softening of nanograined
materials and deviation of strength from the classical Hall-Petch relationship. In this Letter, grain size
dependences of GB migration in nanograined Ag, Cu, and Ni under tension were investigated
quantitatively in a wide size range. As grain size decreases from submicron, GB migration intensifies
and then diminishes below a critical grain size. The GB migration peaks at about 80, 75, and 38 nm in Ag,
Cu, and Ni, respectively. The suppression of GB migration below a critical size can be attributed to GB
relaxation during sample processing or by postthermal annealing. With relaxed GBs the governing
deformation mechanism of nanograins shifts from GB migration to formation of through-grain twins or
stacking faults. GB relaxation, analogous to GB segregation, offers a novel approach to stabilizing
nanograined materials under mechanical loading.
DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.122.126101
The properties of polycrystalline solids are influenced by
grain boundaries (GBs), and hence are grain size depen-
dent. The size dependences of various physical and
mechanical properties become more pronounced as grains
are refined into the nanometer scale due to the substantially
increased density of GBs [1,2]. Additionally, experimental
and simulation studies revealed that the structures and
behaviors of GBs themselves are also size dependent on the
nanometer scale [3–5]. It offers an alternative dimension to
tailor material properties on the nanoscale, drawing increas-
ing interest in solid state physics and materials science in
the past decades [6–10].
In classical models of mechanical properties of solids,
GBs are often treated as “static” geometrical barriers to
dislocation motion. Based on this, a well-known grain size
dependence of strength is derived, predicting increased
strength with decreased grain size, namely, the Hall-Petch
relationship [11,12]. However, deviating from this relation-
ship, some nanograined metals exhibit softening with
decreasing grain size [1,13,14]. One of the fundamental
reasons is that GBs move in response to mechanical loading
with concomitant coarsening of nanograins, especially for
pure metals, as evidenced by experiments and by atomistic
simulations in a number of nanograined metals and alloys
under various loading conditions such as tension [15–17],
compression [18], and indentation [19,20], even at cryo-
genic temperatures. The mechanically induced GB migra-
tion not only deteriorates properties of nanograined
materials but also hinders their processing by plastic
deformation.
The intrinsic mechanism of the mechanically driven GB
migration is still in debate. Several mechanisms have been
proposed [21–27] including the stress-coupling model
[25,26] and the dislocation model [27]. Molecular dynam-
ics simulations showed that under mechanical loading GBs
migrate through obvious atomic shuffling in the boundary
region and emission of dislocations [28], suggesting the
process is dependent of GB structure and composition. This
is supported by a recent experimental observation that with
segregation of Mo at GBs of Ni nanograins of a few
nanometers in size [29], GB mobility is effectively sup-
pressed under mechanical loading [29].
Recently, an abnormal grain size dependence of thermal
stability was identified in nanograined pure Cu and Ni
samples. Thermal GB stability in Cu drops with grain size
decreasing from submicrometers to about
7 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }
. Below
7 0 \ \mathrm { n m }
, GB stability increases as grain size decreases.
The reversed thermal stability below a critical size origi-
nates from an autonomous GB relaxation accompanied by a
GB energy drop during plastic straining of nanograined
metals [30]. It is, therefore, crucial to exploit the grain size
dependence of GB migration and the effect of GB relax-
ation under mechanical loading in nanograined metals.
In this study gradient nanograined (GNG) samples were
used as in the previous study on thermal stability [30]. With
the unique strain delocalization mechanism, gradient nano-
grained structures enable very large tensile strains without
failure in the nanograined layer [15,31] that offers an
opportunity for measuring the mechanical GB mobility in a
wide grain size spectrum.

For producing GNG structures, coarse-grained Cu dog-
bone-shaped tensile specimens with a purity of
were processed using a surface mechanical grinding treat-
ment (SMGT) at cryogenic temperatures (processing
details in Refs. [30,32]). After the treatment, a GNG
surface layer of the gauge section is generated while the
sample core is coarse grained: average transversal grain
sizes increase from
4 2 \pm 1 1 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
(with an aspect ratio of 1.7)
in the topmost surface to
2 0 0 \ \mathrm { n m }
at a depth of
7 0 \ \mu \mathrm { { m } }
Detailed microstructures were described in Ref. [30]. No
change in texture was found from the topmost surface to
4 0 \ \mu \mathrm { { m } }
depth, and processing-induced contaminations
(such as carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen) were not detected
in the surface layer deeper than
0 . 5 \ \mu \mathrm { m }
from the processed
surface. Measurement data from the topmost
0 . 5 \ \mu \mathrm { m }
thick
layer were removed from our analysis to avoid negligible
contamination and possible surface effects.
As revealed previously [30], in the as-prepared SMGT
sample (Cu-1) GB relaxation occurred in the top surface
layer of roughly
2 0 \ \mu \mathrm { m }
thick in which grains are smaller
than about
7 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }
. In deeper layers with grains larger than
7 0 \mathrm { n m }
, no obvious GB relaxation was detected. Therefore,
the GB relaxation effect on mechanical GB migration can
be examined by simple quasistatic uniaxial tension of the
GNG Cu samples at room temperature (Fig. S1 [32]). After
tension, the nanograined surface layer deforms uniformly
with the coarse-grained core to a uniform elongation of
without any surface cracking or delaminating. In cross
sections of the tensioned specimens obvious grain coars-
ening was observed in the surface layer of
7 0 \ \mu \mathrm { { m } }
depth
(Fig. 1). Deeper than
7 0 \ \mu \mathrm { { m } }
coarsening is hardly identified.
The grain coarsening layer was verified by a microhardness
drop (softening), in contrast to hardening in the core
with coarse grains, consistent with previous observations
[22,36]. Statistical measurements under TEM showed that
the average grain size in the topmost layer (in a depth
span of
1 { - } 2 \ \mu \mathrm { m } ,
) increases from
to
4 8 \pm 1 3 ~ \mathrm { n m }
after tension [Figs. 1(c), 1(d), Fig. S2 [32] ]. But in the
subsurface layer coarsening is evident, from
to
1 7 0 \pm 4 1 ~ \mathrm { n m }
at a depth of
3 0 \ \mu \mathrm { { m } }
[Figs. 1(e), 1(f)]. As the
tensile strain is identical (0.31) in the entire specimen, one
may derive the GB migration at different grain sizes with a
given strain in one sample. According to the grain size
profiles measured along depth before and after tension
[Fig. 1(g)], the change in grain size peaks at a depth of
1 5 \ \mu \mathrm { m }
with a maximum value of
[Fig. 1(i)]. It drops
with a decreasing depth below
1 5 \ \mu \mathrm { m }
and becomes
negligible at the topmost surface. A similar grain coars-
ening phenomenon was also observed as the sample was
tensioned at 77 K (Fig. S3 [32]) [37].
Another sample (Cu-2) with larger nanograins in the
topmost surface was prepared by using the same SMGT
process at lower strain rates [32]. The average transversal
grain size is
6 4 \pm 2 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
in the surface and increases to

observed suppression of GB migration in the topmost

or grain size gradient.
Plotting the measured relative grain size change as a
function of the initial grain size, one may find the data from
the two sets of sample (Cu-1 and Cu-2) overlap very well
[Fig. 2(a)], showing a consistent grain size dependence of GB
migration. An increasing GB migration begins at a grain size
of
2 0 0 \ \mathrm { n m }
and increases from 200 to about
7 5 \ \mathrm { n m }
, in
agreement qualitatively with the reported data and analysis in
the literatures [17,25,38]. However, a deviation from the
trend appears below
7 5 \ \mathrm { n m }
and GB migration decreases
sharply with reducing sizes. No obvious coarsening is
observed as grain size approaches
4 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
Comparing with the grain size dependence of thermal
stability [Fig. 2(b)], one may see the deviating grain size of
the mechanical GB migration is very close to the critical
size for thermal stability upturn in the gradient nanograined
Cu sample. It implies a correspondence between the

The same experiment was performed in pure Ag

The difference may be attributed to that thermal stability is
governed by GB migration via thermal activation of atoms
at GBs while the mechanical GB migration is controlled by
atomic motion mediated by dislocation slip. It implies that
the effects of GB relaxation are different on the GB
migration kinetics with and without dislocation mediation.
The difference might be more obvious in metals with high
stacking fault energy (SFE) in which suppression of full

SFE, the peak size is about

Microstructures of the coarsening layer were characterized
under TEM. For the initial grains larger than
7 5 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
in Cu,
typical submicron-sized grains containing some dislocations
with distinct GBs are formed after tension [Fig. 3(c)], without
twin or stacking fault. In contrast, in plenty of grains with
initial sizes below
7 5 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
[as circled in Fig. 3(a)], through-
grain twins and/or stacking faults were detected [Fig. 3(b)].
Twins and/or stacking faults in other grains (not circled) were
seen by tilting the sample. Twin thickness ranges from a few
to ten nanometers, much smaller than that in conventionally-
deformed Cu
{ \sim } 4 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }
[42]), meaning a very high local shear
stress applied. Twins exist in some very fine nanograins
before tension. But the statistic twin density increases
tremendously after tension at room temperature.
In the tensioned Ni sample, coarsening of large grains
\left( > 4 0 \ \mathrm { n m } \right)
is dominated by GB migration, analogous to that
in Cu. For grains smaller than
3 8 ~ \mathrm { n m }
, slightly coarsening
was observed after tension but many more stacking faults in
almost each nanograin [Figs. 3(d), 3(e)] in comparison with
that prior to tension, suggesting formation of stacking faults
activated by very high shear stresses dominates plastic
PHYSICAL REVIEW LETTERS 122, 126101 (2019)
deformation. These evidences point out that suppression of
the mechanically induced GB migration in these metals
corresponds to a deformation mechanism shift to deforma-
tion twinning and/or stacking faults as grains are smaller
than a critical size.
Both experimental and computational examples showed
that plastic deformation can relax structures of GBs in
nanograined materials [43,44]. Emission of partial disloca-
tions from a GB, forming either stacking faults or twins in
neighboring grains, was proposed to trigger GB relaxation
via GB dissociation processes to lower energy states [45–47].
It provides a general mode of GB relaxation especially in
metals with low stacking fault energy like Cu. GB energy can
be lowered due to formation of nanometer-scale twins from
the GB. Chen et al. [48] observed that atomic diffusion is
remarkably retarded and GB electromigration slowed down
along the GBs adjacent to the triple point where they meet a
twin boundary in Cu.
GBs can also be relaxed by thermal annealing under
proper conditions. The as-prepared SMGT nanograined
sample (Cu-1) was heated rapidly to
2 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
and held for
15 min for inducing GB relaxation of nanograins with sizes
around
8 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }
in the subsurface layer
1 5 \mathrm { - } 2 0 ~ \mu \mathrm { m }
deep).
After annealing, no obvious change was detected in average
grain size (from
to
8 2 \pm 2 5 ~ \mathrm { n m } ,
) and in morphol-
ogy [Figs. 4(a), 4(b)]. The thermal stability of this layer is
much elevated, indicative of a relaxation process of GBs
during annealing [46]. The annealed sample (Cu-1T) was
tensioned to a uniform strain of 0.31 under the same
condition of
\mathrm { C u - 1 }
. Interestingly, after tension there is no
detectable grain size change and the average size remains
8 4 \pm 2 6 ~ \mathrm { n m }
[Fig. 4(c)], distinct from that of the corre-
sponding layer in sample Cu-1, in which the average size
increases to
1 3 8 \pm 3 9 ~ \mathrm { n m }
after tension. Under close TEM
observations, plenty of twins were found in nanograins
after tension, as circled in Fig. 4(c). By tilting the samples,
twins or stacking faults can be seen in many other grains.
The consistent results in two different sets of experi-
ments indicate that the mechanical GB migration is
effectively suppressed by GB relaxation, induced either
mechanically or thermally [46,48,49], while the dominant
plastic deformation mechanism switches to formation of
twins and stacking faults in nanograins. The deformation
mechanism change agrees with earlier observations. The
mechanical coarsening becomes reluctant in Co-P nano-
grained alloys when deformation is dominated by twinning
or formation of stacking faults, while the coarsening is
obvious as deformation is governed by full dislocation
activity in a nanograined Ni-Fe sample [47]. With segre-
gation of Mo at GBs, the mechanically induced coarsening
of Ni nanograins is suppressed and the deformation
mechanism is substituted by generation of stacking faults
inside the Ni nanograins [29].
Apparently, generation of twins or stacking faults from
GBs is a key process for GB relaxation that influences the

mechanical GB migration. This can be understood by
comparing the critical shear stress needed to nucleate a
perfect dislocation with that required to initiate Shockley
partial dislocations, with an approximation of the source
size equal to the grain size
. The comparison can be
made in terms of the Orowan relation [50] in the classical
dislocation theory. Full dislocation multiplication requires
that Frank-Read-type sources have a minimum grain size
at the yield strength
( \sigma _ { y } )
which is grain size depen-
dent; i.e., bowing out of full dislocations is inhibited as
grain sizes are smaller than
while initiation of partial
dislocations is preferred:

Based on the measured yield strength of the nanograined
metals [32], the critical sizes were calculated, being
PHYSICAL REVIEW LETTERS 122, 126101 (2019)
, and
4 3 ~ \mathrm { n m }
, and
, 35, and
2 2 \mathrm { n m }
for
Ag, Cu, and Ni, respectively.
values for
\mathrm { C u }
and Ni are
very close to that reported in Ref. [51]. The calculated
values are approximately in agreement with the measured

calculated

noncoarsening stable grain sizes
\Delta D = 0
Although the classical dislocation model used here is
very simplified, the agreement between the calculated
results and the experimental observations are surprisingly
satisfactory. The minor differences between them may be
attributed to the slight texture in the nanograins, inaccuracy
of the input parameters (especially the grain size depend-
ence of stress), and measurement errors in grain sizes.
Hence, the suppression of the mechanically induced GB
migration with GB relaxation below a critical size can be
reasonably attributed to inhibition of full dislocations and
nucleation of partials to form twins or stacking faults in
nanograins. The observed GB relaxation effect is funda-
mentally related to the interface phenomenon in thin films
and nanolaminates widely presented in electronic devices.
Analogous to the GB relaxation in pure metals, formation of
a low-energy interface in bilayer heterostructures has been
observed in the literatures as their sizes are reduced down to
the nanometer scale, forming more stable interfaces, as
observed in Al=Pb [52] and
\mathrm { { C u / N b } }
[53] nanolaminates.
An important implication of this finding is that very
fine nanograins even in pure metals can be mechanically
stable as long as GBs are relaxed mechanically or ther-
mally. Thus, structures of metals could be further refined
and stabilized below the critical sizes with plastic
deformation. GB relaxation offers a novel strategy stabi-
lizing nanograined metals under mechanical stimuli.
